Story:
The cruise ship my husband & I were on had just pulled out of port when we heard loud voices below us. The captain and a man in black were arguing. We tried to listen.
The captain told ~~the~~ man he would have to go back to port to have him taken off the ship. The man argued that he could pay the fare in cash (here and there). But the captain said he could not do that. The man started to run saying I won't go back, you can't make me.
We felt the ship turning around heading towards the port. When we docked the police came on board searching for the man. An hour later they found him, he had been wanted for a crime, and the police finally had caught him.

Story: James Garner and Lana Turner are at the casino in Monte Carlo listening to José Greco entertain on the flamenco guitar. They have just come from a gala at Prince Rainier's home. They are both tired and bored with the chit-chat they have been listening to and participating in. Both are hiding their feelings behind masks of gaiety. But both are really just wishing they could go home, take off their shoes, drink beer, and watch TV. But they can't because they are expected to love the exciting life of the international movie star. Actually, they envy their friends who don't have to look handsome and beautiful all day long—who can enjoy simple pleasures of life, in privacy, with their friends + family.

I know he's your "best friend" but when you booked passage for you and your "best friend" we didn't ~~expect~~ a DOG! You say you have a second best friend — a bird in a cage! Well, I guess we can compromise — yes you can have a bird in your cabin — a pelican! Oh my goodness! Now, sir....

Story: She was his love, he adored her every move, her every smile or touch. For a long while she felt the same toward him but gradually over the months she came to realize she really needed someone more exciting. She planned to tell him tonight but his thoughtfullness at choosing such a romantic spot for drinks and dinner made her think twice. Surely any man who knew all her favorite songs and managed to get the roaming singer to play exclusively for her was worth much more than a daring, devil-may-care person. Maybe, she thought, I'll tell him about my doubts and we can work it out together.

Story:
He sat at the drafting board feeling particularly disenchanted with his work. Staring at the picture of his wife, ten year old son and five year old daughter, he recognized the necessarily contradictory yearnings running through his mind.
    After twelve years of marriage and thirty seven on the planet, he had acquired the trappings of familial and vocational respectability.
    There was security in each area, if he wanted it. Yet he thought that he was living a life as someone else's employee and participating in a marriage not as fulfilling as he might have hoped.

ensemble PowIm Explanation: Criterion 1a/1d: The captain actively tries to influence and deceive the policeman to avoid detection for smuggling, a clear attempt to maintain power over the situation and avoid consequences. He tells lies, changes the subject, and claims authority ('he knows everything that's going on on the ship') to control the investigation.
ensemble Pa+ Explanation: The captain claims 'he knows everything that's going on on the ship,' asserting an authoritative, almost omniscient position of expertise, which is prestige-enhancing in this context (expertise-based prestige).
ensemble Pa- Explanation: No pejorative description of the captain; the story merely relates his actions without lowering his prestige through narrator description.
ensemble N Explanation: No explicit desire statement like 'wants to' or 'hopes to' — the need to avoid capture is inferred from his deceptive actions, not stated.
ensemble Act Explanation: Act-: The captain actively tells the policeman he didn't smuggle, explains, changes the subject, and lies about his wife's health — all instrumental actions toward his power goal. However, the effort ultimately fails as the police search anyway.
ensemble Bw Explanation: The police are in the process of getting a search warrant, but this is part of the initial conflict and not an obstacle that emerges after the captain's goal is established.
ensemble SA Explanation: No character explicitly anticipates the outcome of the deception; the story only describes his actions without internal foresight.
ensemble Fa Explanation: No affective state described for the captain regarding the outcome of his power attempt.
ensemble F+ Explanation: No positive emotional reaction from others described.
ensemble F- Explanation: No negative emotional reaction from others described.
ensemble Eff Explanation: The police get a search warrant anyway and find the cocaine, which is a specific counterreaction and concrete consequence of the captain's failed deception. The discovery of the cocaine and the revelation that the captain knew about it is a distinct effect.
ensemble HopeFear Explanation: Fear of Power, criterion 3: The captain's power attempt is shown to be deceptive (he lies about his wife's illness, claims ignorance) and ultimately flawed, as the police see through it and obtain the warrant. The outcome undermines his power, illustrating power as deceptive and unsuccessful.
ensemble PowIm Majority Explanation:
Criterion 1a/1d: The captain actively tries to influence and deceive the policeman to avoid detection for smuggling, a clear attempt to maintain power over the situation and avoid consequences. He tells lies, changes the subject, and claims authority ('he knows everything that's going on on the ship') to control the investigation.
Criterion 1d (persuading, arguing, trying to influence): the captain is actively trying to convince the policeman of his innocence by lying, changing the subject, and claiming he wouldn't be that stupid. This is a clear attempt to have impact on another person to maintain his own freedom and control over his illegal operation.

There is no forceful action, attempt to influence or control others, no strong emotional impact on others, and no explicit concern for personal or institutional prestige as a form of power. The mention of 'the future of the institution' is a vague institutional concern, not an active attempt to establish, maintain, or restore power over a specific person or group. Under Rule A, this is an achievement/social-contribution story, not a power story. No power goal can be identified.
No power goal identified. The story describes a scientific discovery (achievement) and speculates about its possible social implications, but no character is shown establishing, maintaining, or restoring impact, control, or influence over another person, group, or the world at large. The phrase 'MAYBE IT WILL HELP THE EQUAL RIGHTS MOVEMENT' is not a stated desire to influence, and selling or giving away the cure is routine dissemination, not a power action.

The discovery itself is not a power action; there is no described forceful action, unsolicited intervention, persuasion, or impression management.
No power imagery. 'Helping the equal rights movement' is altruistic, not an attempt to control or influence for power's sake. Selling or giving away a cure is a distributional choice, not a power action. The concern about the institution's future reflects organizational survival, not personal reputation or power concern.
No explicit power goal identified. The characters are concerned about a scientific discovery that could help the equal rights movement and secure the future of their institution, but this is an achievement-oriented concern (the discovery and its potential benefits) not a power concern about establishing, maintaining, or restoring control, influence, or impact over another person, group, or the world at large. There is no forceful action, no impression management, no unsolicited advice, no emotional impact on others, and no active pursuit of reputation or position. The potential sale or gifting of the cure is a routine commercial or generous act, not a power action. The story does not meet any of the three Power Imagery criteria.

Story: Eli sat at his drafting board and shifted his eyes from the engineering design, to the ocean beyond the window and then to the picture on the drafting table. What were his goals in life? Was it worth it to leave his family in Israel and come 6000 miles to seek his fortune in America? Would his marriage to an American girl give him the roots, the security, the peace and the progeny he dreamed of? To escape these doubts he turned his eyes back to the drafting table and buried himself in his work. He had an idea for a new air pollution control device that might be patentable. Damn it! He could never get it right. He closed his eyes to look inward to find the dream or the design. Suddenly a flush of red exploded in his brain and he slumped, dead, on the drafting board.

Story:
What has Cambridge meant to me? Why did I feel so stifled and so desperate to leave it — and yet I now look back on those Cambridge days as, if not the happiest, at least the richest in my life.
Suddenly I remember an evening with Peter, my boyfriend in sophomore year. We walked up Mem Drive in the pouring rain, I wore my jaunty felt hat, we sang french songs as we walked home in the downpour. This kind of memory is what makes me feel my 30 years.
The other day I met David another Cambridge beau in the street. He was still in the searching period of his life. I, [at?] 30, was struggling to get my son home in a straight line, just ~~trying~~ trying to survive.

Story: Eli once took me to this super plastic restaurant in New York where the waiters wore serapes and pointed Mexican straw hats and one guy came around + serenaded us with a guitar. He sang all of Eli's childhood favorites from Argentina, "Malaguena Salerosa" and "Adios Muchachos". I knew all the words from the endless car trips to Vermont to inspect dirty smokestacks in the Green Mountains. I associated those songs with a very superficial but nice kind of good time we used to have. That's why it was so weird when Becky, ten years old, brought me a gift last year of her newly dead mother's record. I put it on and heard, Malaguena Salerosa and Adios Muchachos. It was ghoulish and sad that these two dead shared their favorite songs, and was the beginning of a strong and macabre bond between Becky + me.
